text: The surgeon uses three ports on the left side, including a submemory incision and one in the axilla, to access the anterior mediastinum. The mediastinal pleura is opened anterior to the left phrenic nerve, and the lump on the left thymic lobe is identified. The surgeon lifts the mass inferiorly and opens the pleura of the fibrous pericardium to release it.

The surgeon dissects the thymic mass from underneath, carefully lifting it off the pulmonary trunk and the ascending and arch of the aorta. The dissection reveals the vein and artery draining into and out of the left internal mammary, with caution taken to avoid injuring the left phrenic nerve.

The surgeon retracts the left biomic horn extending into the neck, revealing the aggressive and invasive appearance of the tumor. Cautious dissection is performed around the innominate vein to establish a clear surgical plane.

The surgeon uses a hook to support the tissue and a bipolar fenestrated instrument to dissect towards the thyroid, revealing vessels at the top of the thymic horn. The vessels are clipped for safety before being divided with the bipolar and hook.

The surgeon releases the right thymus horn and approaches the confluence of the innominate veins into the superior vena cava (SVC). Dissection is performed carefully around the large vessels using a hemlock clip.

The surgeon isolates the vein draining into the superior vena cava (SVC) while carefully avoiding injury to the right phrenic nerve, which descends from the right internal mammary base onto the lateral side of the SVC. Blunt dissection is used extensively, and a lymph node is visible. The surgeon uses a bipolar device to safely divide the tissue without conducting electricity to the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon opens the right pleura to enhance visualization, then divides the remaining tissue. An endocatch bag is used to safely contain the specimen, preventing any contamination of the left pleura.

The surgeon inserts an endocatch bag through a twelve millimeter port in the axilla, closes the bag, and pulls it through. The innominate vein and aorta are visibly clear of any tissue following the tumor resection, with a comparison image showing the area before and after the procedure.
